steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The sentence has “fellow OECD member” (a noun phrase) followed by “nation” (a noun) and then “Latvia” (a proper noun). This creates a structure where “Latvia” is in apposition to “nation”—meaning it identifies or specifies which nation is being discussed.

steps.title: Apply the appositive punctuation rule
steps.content: The rule is: a restrictive appositive (essential to identifying the noun) takes no punctuation; a non-restrictive appositive (extra information) uses commas or dashes. Here, “Latvia” is essential to identify which of the 38 OECD member nations is meant, making this restrictive and requiring no punctuation between “nation” and “Latvia”. Choices B, C, and D violate this rule by adding unnecessary punctuation. That matches choice A—A is correct.
